questions.question: Explain the difference between kill() and raise() functions with examples.
keyPoints: kill() sends signal to another process or process group using PID
raise() sends signal to the calling process itself
Example: kill(1234, SIGTERM) sends SIGTERM to process 1234; raise(SIGTERM) sends SIGTERM to itself
raise(sig) is equivalent to kill(getpid(), sig)

questions.question: What happens when kill() is called with signal number 0? Explain its use.
keyPoints: Signal 0 (null signal) performs error checking without sending any signal
Returns 0 if process exists and sender has permission
Returns -1 with ESRCH if process doesn't exist
Returns -1 with EPERM if permission denied
Used to check if a process is alive without affecting it

questions.question: Differentiate between SIGTERM and SIGKILL signals.
keyPoints: SIGTERM (15) is a request for termination, can be caught/handled
SIGKILL (9) forces immediate termination, cannot be caught/ignored
SIGTERM allows graceful cleanup (close files, release resources)
SIGKILL is immediate termination without cleanup
SIGKILL is used when process doesn't respond to SIGTERM

questions.question: Explain the return values of kill() function on success and failure.
keyPoints: On success, kill() returns 0
On failure, kill() returns -1
errno set to ESRCH if no such process exists
errno set to EPERM if permission denied
errno set to EINVAL if invalid signal number

questions.question: What happens when pid argument of kill() is negative? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: When pid < 0 and pid != -1, signal sent to process group |pid|
pid = -1 sends signal to all processes sender has permission to
Example: kill(-1000, SIGTERM) sends to all processes in group 1000
kill(-1, SIGTERM) sends to all except init process

questions.question: Write a C program to demonstrate use of raise() function for graceful termination.
keyPoints: Include signal.h and stdio.h headers
Define signal handler for SIGTERM
Use raise(SIGTERM) to send signal to self
Handler performs cleanup before exit
Program demonstrates signal handling mechanism

questions.question: Explain signal disposition and mention the three valid actions for signal handling.
keyPoints: Signal disposition defines what happens when signal is delivered
SIG_DFL: default action for the signal
SIG_IGN: ignore the signal
Custom handler: user-defined function to handle signal
Each signal has a default action: terminate, stop, continue, or core dump

questions.question: Explain the kill() system call in detail including its syntax, parameters, return values, and various use cases with different pid values.
keyPoints: Syntax: int kill(pid_t pid, int sig)
pid > 0: send to process with that PID
pid = 0: send to all processes in caller's process group
pid = -1: send to all processes sender has permission (except init)
pid < 0 and != -1: send to process group |pid|
sig = 0: perform error checking only (no signal sent)
Return: 0 on success, -1 on failure with errno
Use cases: process termination, inter-process communication, checking process existence

questions.question: Write a C program where parent process sends SIGUSR1 signal to child process, and child handles it by printing a message and terminating gracefully.
keyPoints: Use fork() to create child process
Parent uses kill() to send SIGUSR1 to child
Child sets signal handler using signal() for SIGUSR1
Handler prints message and calls exit()
Parent waits for child using wait()
Demonstrates inter-process signal communication

questions.question: Explain signal handling mechanism in Unix. Discuss signal(), sigaction(), and the concept of signal masks.
keyPoints: Signals are software interrupts for process notification
signal() sets handler for a specific signal
sigaction() is more robust alternative to signal()
Signal disposition: default, ignore, or custom handler
Signal mask: set of blocked signals using sigprocmask()
Pending signals: delivered when unblocked
Safe signal handling: only async-signal-safe functions allowed

questions.question: Write a detailed note on different types of signals in Unix/Linux with their default actions and common use cases.
keyPoints: SIGHUP(1): Hangup - terminate or reload configuration
SIGINT(2): Interrupt - generated by Ctrl+C
SIGQUIT(3): Quit - generated by Ctrl+\, core dump
SIGKILL(9): Kill - forced termination (cannot be caught)
SIGTERM(15): Termination - graceful termination request
SIGSTOP(19): Stop - suspend execution (cannot be caught)
SIGCONT(18): Continue - resume stopped process
SIGUSR1(10) & SIGUSR2(12): User-defined signals
SIGSEGV(11): Segmentation fault - invalid memory access
SIGFPE(8): Floating point exception
